planting.note: Plant in spring in a cool, moist shade bed.

directions: Set the crown level with rich, consistently moist soil in shade. This selection produces very narrow, cross-banded fronds that give an unusual crested, latticed look. Keep it evenly moist so the distinctive slender fronds develop well.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist shade for full crests.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in damp, humus-rich woodland ground. The tips of the fronds and pinnae divide into frilled crests for a soft, tasseled appearance. Maintain even moisture in shade and the crests fill out through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a moist, shaded border.

directions: Set the crown level with rich, evenly moist soil in partial to full shade. Finely divided, lacy green fronds rise on reddish stems for a delicate, airy clump. Keep the root zone moist and the feathery fronds stay full into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sheltered shade for best contrast.

directions: Plant the crown flush with cool, moist, well-drained humus in a shaded, sheltered spot. New fronds emerge chartreuse to pale silver-green with striking dark maroon-red stems. Provide even moisture and shade to keep the stem-to-frond contrast sharp.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a shaded, evenly moist bed.

directions: Site this narrow, upright selection where its slim vertical fronds can stand against a shady wall or path. Keep the crown at soil level and mulch to conserve moisture. Its finely cut, tapering fronds emerge in spring and form a tidy columnar clump.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring in cool, moist shade.

directions: Plant with the crown flush to the soil in rich, moisture-retentive ground. The narrow pinnae end in tasseled crests, so give it a spot where the lacy tips can be seen up close. Fronds flush lacy and finely divided in spring and remain airy all season.

planting.note: Set divisions out in spring in moist woodland soil.

directions: Plant the crown at the soil surface and mulch with leaf litter to mimic a woodland floor. Golden-brown scales coat the emerging stalks, most striking as the fiddleheads uncurl in spring. The robust fronds are near-evergreen in milder zones and form a broad vase-shaped clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sheltered, shaded spot.

directions: Give this stately crested form room to display its arching, tassel-tipped fronds. Set the crown level with the soil in rich, moist ground and shelter it from drying wind. Each frond and pinna ends in a neat crest, unfurling in spring into an imposing symmetrical clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring in deep, rich, consistently moist shade.

directions: Give this giant native the moist, deep woodland soil it needs and plenty of room to spread. Plant the crown level with the soil and water generously through dry spells. Its broad, arching fronds can top four feet, making it one of the largest wood ferns for shade.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist, acidic woodland soil.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in humus-rich, acidic ground under deciduous trees. The lacy, twice-cut fronds stay green into winter, giving cold gardens off-season texture. It naturalizes slowly into graceful evergreen colonies.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, shaded, acidic soil.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in cool, damp shade and mulch with leaf mold. Its broad, triangular, dark green fronds arch outward from a stout crown into a large clump. Fronds flush in spring and persist late into the year in mild climates.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, cool shade.

directions: This dainty crested form has finely divided fronds tipped with small tassels, best shown at the front of a shade bed. Plant the crown at soil level and keep the ground evenly moist. Lacy fronds emerge in spring and give a delicate, feathery texture all season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, partial shade.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in humus-rich, evenly moist ground. Dark, shaggy scales clothe the stems and set off the narrow, arching, leathery fronds. Fronds emerge in spring and form a neat, upright vase-shaped clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, shaded spot with steady moisture.

directions: This distinctive fern has few, very wide, leathery pinnae that give it an almost tropical look. Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, moist ground sheltered from cold wind. Its bold blue-green fronds are evergreen in mild zones and add strong contrast among finer-textured shade plants.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich shade.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in fertile, moisture-retentive ground under high shade. The glossy, upright, leathery fronds hold their color and stay largely evergreen in mild winters. It forms a full, vase-shaped clump that tolerates heat better than many wood ferns.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet shade.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in rich, reliably moist ground; it tolerates wet feet well. Its narrow, strongly upright fronds form a tight vertical clump like a green fountain. The slim, columnar habit makes it useful for adding height in a small shady space.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, rich, partly shaded ground.

directions: This tall natural hybrid wants moist, fertile soil and room to rise; plant the crown at the surface and water freely. Its narrow, upright fronds can reach four to five feet, giving strong vertical structure in a shade border. Sterile and non-spreading, it stays as a well-behaved single clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet to moist, shaded soil.

directions: This tall native hybrid likes damp, rich ground; plant the crown at the surface and keep it moist. Its upright fronds rise well above most native wood ferns, adding height to a shady, wet border. Fronds flush in spring and stand tall through the growing season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in rich, moist shade.

directions: This vigorous male-fern hybrid grows tall and full; give it fertile, moisture-retentive ground and set the crown at soil level. The large, arching fronds form a bold shuttlecock and are near-evergreen in mild zones. Golden scales clothe the emerging stalks in spring as the fiddleheads uncurl.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, humus-rich shade.

directions: Plant the stout crown level with the soil in rich, evenly moist ground. The broad, tapering fronds radiate symmetrically from a thick woody rootstock into a handsome shuttlecock. Emerging in spring with scaly stalks, it makes a bold, sculptural clump for the shade garden.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist woodland shade.

directions: This slender selection has narrow, gracefully tapering fronds; plant the crown at soil level in rich, moist ground. Golden-brown scales coat the emerging stalks in spring for early-season interest. It forms a refined, upright clump narrower than the typical species.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sheltered, moist shade.

directions: Grown for its bright new growth, this fern flushes soft golden-green fronds that mature to green. Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, moist ground sheltered from cold wind. Site it where spring and summer light can highlight the glowing fresh fronds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sheltered, moist, partial shade.

directions: New fronds emerge in warm coppery-orange and pink tones before settling to green, giving the fern its name. Plant the crown at soil level in rich, moist ground and shelter it from hard frost. Recurring flushes of colorful new growth appear through the season in mild climates.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich shade.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in fertile, evenly moist ground under high shade. Its uniform, narrow, dark green fronds form a tidy, upright clump. Dark scales clothe the stalks as the fronds emerge in spring, and they stay largely evergreen in mild zones.

planting.note: Set out in spring in cool, shaded, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this glossy, leathery fern in shade with the crown just at soil level in soil that drains freely. It tolerates lime and drier shade better than many ferns once established. The dark green fronds are largely evergreen, so remove only damaged foliage as fresh croziers rise in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shaded, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this fern partial to full shade and rich, evenly moist soil, planting the crown level with the surface. New fronds emerge with a distinctive arching, tassel-like curl and a lustrous dark green sheen. Keep it out of drying wind and refresh the mulch each spring to hold moisture around the roots.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade; suits pots and shaded rock crevices.

directions: This compact fern thrives in shade with cool, humus-rich, well-drained soil and looks striking in containers or shaded rockeries. Set the crown at grade and keep the roots evenly moist without letting them sit wet. Fine dark fronds with contrasting near-black stems stay evergreen in milder zones; tidy old foliage in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, shaded woodland soil.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in fertile, moisture-retentive soil with the crown at the surface. The emerging fronds carry a soft metallic sheen that matures to glossy green. Provide steady moisture and a spring mulch, and remove only worn fronds as new growth appears.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade with good drainage.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in well-drained, humus-rich soil, keeping the crown at grade. The stiff, leathery fronds are notably rigid and glossy, giving strong evergreen structure to a shade bed. Water through establishment and dry spells, and remove tattered fronds in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sheltered, moist shade.

directions: Give this Australian fern a sheltered, shaded position with cool, moisture-retentive soil and plant the crown at the surface. It forms plantlets along the fronds that root where they touch the ground, gradually spreading the clump. Keep it evenly watered and protect from hard frost in colder gardens.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, gritty, well-drained shade.

directions: This tough alpine fern needs cool conditions and sharply drained, gritty soil, ideally in a shaded rock garden. Set the crown at grade and avoid summer heat and stagnant moisture, which it resents. The narrow, spiny, once-cut fronds stay evergreen; tidy any winter damage in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade with excellent drainage.

directions: A western North American relative of the sword fern, this species tolerates drier, rockier shade than most and wants very free drainage. Plant the crown at grade on a slope or in gritty soil and water sparingly once established. Its overlapping leaflets give a tidy, upright look, and it needs only a light spring cleanup.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, shaded woodland soil.

directions: Plant in cool, moist shade in humus-rich soil with the crown set at the surface. This Pacific Northwest fern produces bulblets near the frond tips that can start new plants. Keep the soil consistently moist, especially in summer, and remove worn fronds as new growth emerges in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shaded, well-drained soil.

directions: Give this west-coast fern partial to full shade and well-drained soil with the crown at grade. It appreciates summer moisture but tolerates some dryness once mature in cooler coastal conditions. The arching evergreen fronds suit woodland edges; tidy the clump each spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained shade.

directions: This low, dense fern from Japan carries stiff, spiny, yellow-green fronds that stay evergreen and hold their form well. Plant in partial shade in gritty, free-draining soil with the crown at the surface. It resents wet winter soil, so ensure good drainage and water moderately through the growing season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shaded, humus-rich soil.

directions: This selection carries low, spreading, finely divided fronds with a soft, feathery texture, excellent for shaded groundcover. Plant in partial to full shade in fertile, well-drained soil at grade and keep evenly moist. Cut the old fronds back in late winter so the fresh spring flush shows to best effect.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shaded, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Prized for its extremely finely cut, moss-like fronds that overlap into a dense, soft mound, this cultivar wants shade and rich, moist soil. Plant the crown level with the surface and shelter it from drying wind. It is sterile and spreads only by division; refresh mulch each spring and remove worn fronds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shaded, humus-rich, moist soil.

directions: This form bears broad, lacy, doubly divided fronds that lie in a low, spreading rosette, often producing bulbils along the midribs. Plant in partial to full shade in fertile, moisture-retentive soil at grade. Peg down a frond onto moist soil to root the plantlets, and tidy old foliage before spring growth.

planting.note: Propagate and plant only by division in spring.

directions: An old and highly regarded selection with narrow, elegantly tapering fronds whose leaflets sweep gracefully toward the tip. Because it sets no viable spores, it is increased only by division, so plant the crown at grade in rich, shaded, well-drained soil. Keep evenly moist and clean up worn fronds as the new arching flush appears.

planting.note: Plant in spring; suits shaded containers and small beds.

directions: A dwarf, congested form whose overlapping leaflets crowd along short, upright fronds, making a neat clump for small shaded spaces or pots. Plant the crown at the surface in rich, well-drained soil and keep it evenly moist. Its compact habit needs little upkeep beyond removing tired fronds in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shaded, fertile, moist soil.

directions: This selection has a more upright, symmetrical shuttlecock habit with softly divided, semi-glossy fronds. Plant in partial to full shade in rich, well-drained soil at grade and keep the roots evenly moist. It makes a reliable, structural clump for the shade border; simply cut back worn fronds before spring growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shaded, well-drained soil; good in pots.

directions: A compact, refined fern with dark, glossy, finely cut fronds that suit shaded rockeries and containers. Plant the crown at grade in humus-rich, well-drained soil and keep steadily moist without waterlogging. It stays evergreen in mild zones, needing only removal of old fronds as fresh growth unfurls.

planting.note: Divide and replant in early spring so the coppery new fronds establish before summer heat.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in wet, humus-rich, acidic soil where the purple-flushed spring fronds catch the light. Never let the root zone dry out, and shelter from harsh afternoon sun. The young fronds emerge wine-purple and mature to green, deepening the display in early spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring at the margin of a pond, bog, or wet woodland.

directions: Site this North American variety in constantly moist, acidic soil in partial shade, setting the crown flush with the ground. Keep it wet, especially in its first two seasons, and it will slowly form a substantial clump. Fertile spore-bearing tips top the fronds in late spring, aging from green to cinnamon brown.

planting.note: Establish divisions in spring in reliably moist, shaded ground.

directions: Plant the crown level with the soil in wet, acidic, organic-rich ground in partial to full shade. Keep moisture constant through summer, as drought scorches the delicate frond tips. Each frond and leaflet ends in a forked, tasseled crest, most striking once fronds fully expand in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, shaded, acidic soil.

directions: Plant the crown flush with the ground in consistently damp, rich, acidic soil in partial shade. Keep the root zone moist year-round, as this East Asian species dislikes drying out. Its bright green spring fronds unfurl from woolly fiddleheads, with separate fertile fronds appearing early in the season.

planting.note: Divide and plant in cool spring weather while the soil stays moist.

directions: Site in dappled shade with steady moisture and never let the crown dry out fully. Work compost or fine bark into the bed to hold water at the root zone. Expect its airy, fan-shaped fronds on wiry dark stalks to flush through spring and hold into fall.

planting.note: Plant slow-spreading divisions in spring to let them colonize a shaded bed.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, well-drained soil where it can slowly creep into a low ground cover. Shelter from harsh afternoon sun and keep the surface mulched. The finely cut fronds emerge pinkish-bronze in spring and often persist as a near-evergreen carpet in mild winters.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a warm, sheltered, shaded pocket.

directions: Plant in gritty, humus-rich soil with reliable moisture and good drainage. Protect the crown from frost in cooler climates or grow it in a container to move under cover. New fronds emerge a coppery rose color before hardening to green through the season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shaded, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow like the species in moist, free-draining, lime-friendly soil in shade. Keep it away from standing water to prevent crown rot. Its strap-like fronds end in forked, frilled crests that add texture to a shady border.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes over shaded rock or ledges in spring.

directions: Drape the creeping rhizomes across mossy boulders or a shaded, gritty bed with sharp drainage. It resents soggy soil, so mimic its natural rock ledges. The small evergreen fronds hold their color over winter and green up again each spring.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost in shade with rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown level in humus-rich, well-drained soil in full to part shade and shelter from cold, drying wind. It tolerates coastal salt and drier shade better than most ferns. The glossy, holly-like leathery fronds stay evergreen in mild zones.

planting.note: Plant after frost in shaded, fertile, free-draining soil.

directions: Grow like the species in rich, well-drained shade, protecting the crown from hard freezes. This selection has broader, more coarsely toothed leaflets than the plain form. Its bold, glossy fronds hold up well as evergreen texture in a shady bed.

planting.note: Plant creeping divisions in spring to form a low ground cover.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic, well-drained soil where the running rhizomes can knit into a low mat. Keep it evenly damp in part shade or cool sun. New fronds emerge reddish-bronze before turning deep green, forming a dense evergreen carpet.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sheltered, moist, shaded spot.

directions: Give this bold fern rich, moist, acidic soil and room to spread by its creeping rhizomes. Shelter it from cold wind and mulch the crown where winters are hard. The large, leathery fronds flush coppery-red before maturing to dark green and can persist through mild winters.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, shaded ground with room to spread.

directions: Site this large fern in consistently moist, humus-rich soil in part shade, ideally near a stream or seep. Give it plenty of space for its broad, arching fronds to develop. The leathery evergreen fronds show the chain-like rows of spore cases along their undersides.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a sheltered, moist, shaded position.

directions: Site in rich, moist, well-drained soil in part shade with shelter from cold wind. Its long, arching fronds root at the tip from a small bulbil to start new plants. Bright red new growth is the main attraction, aging to green over the season.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in autumn as this fern begins its main growing season.

directions: Press the rhizomes into gritty, alkaline, well-drained soil or a shaded wall and keep them from baking in summer. This species leafs out in autumn and often rests in summer heat. Its broad, deeply cut fronds are freshest through the cool, damp months.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, moist soil with afternoon shade.

directions: This reblooming mophead flowers on both old and new wood, so a late freeze rarely erases the whole show. Deadhead spent heads to encourage repeat flushes and feed lightly in spring. Expect blue to pink mopheads from early summer until frost, with color driven by soil pH.

planting.note: Set out in spring in moist, well-drained soil with part shade.

directions: A reblooming lacecap that flowers on old and new growth, giving flat lacy heads with a ring of showy sterile florets. Keep evenly watered and deadhead to prompt fresh flushes through the season. Sturdy red-tinged stems hold pink or blue lacecaps from early summer to fall depending on pH.

planting.note: Plant in spring; suits containers and small beds.

directions: A compact dwarf mophead bred for tidy beds and pots, with strong mildew resistance. Give it rich, moist soil and part shade, and prune only lightly right after bloom. Deep pink to red mophead flowers age to green and appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil with afternoon shade.

directions: A compact reblooming mophead that flowers on old and new wood for reliable color even after a hard winter. Keep the soil evenly moist and acidic for the deepest blue, and remove spent heads to spur reblooming. Rounded mophead flowers open in early summer and continue into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist soil with dappled shade.

directions: A refined mountain hydrangea prized for its airy lacecap heads and good fall foliage color. Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and keep pruning minimal, as it blooms mainly on old wood. Lacecaps show sky-blue centers ringed by pink-to-blue sterile florets from early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; good for small gardens and containers.

directions: A tidy dwarf mountain hydrangea that reblooms on new wood, so it flowers well even in colder zones. Keep the soil consistently moist and site it in morning sun with afternoon shade. Small double lacecap heads in blue to pink appear from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall; blooms earlier than most.

directions: One of the earliest panicle hydrangeas to flower, starting weeks ahead of most cultivars. Give it full sun and prune in late winter to shape and boost bloom size. White panicles open in early summer and quickly turn deep pink to rosy red.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall; can be trained as a small tree.

directions: The old-fashioned large PeeGee, often limbed up into a small flowering tree. Prune in late winter and stake young standards until the trunk thickens. Massive white panicles open in late summer and age to dusty pink, drying well for arrangements.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in sun to part shade.

directions: A late-blooming panicle hydrangea that extends the season into early fall. Prune in late winter to keep it shapely and productive. Open, lacy white panicles appear in late summer, providing bloom when many shrubs have finished.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in full sun.

directions: An upright, strong-stemmed panicle type with dense flower cones that resist flopping. Cut back in late winter to encourage vigorous flowering wood. Full white panicles open in midsummer and transform to a rich pomegranate red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in part shade.

directions: The first pink-flowered smooth hydrangea, blooming reliably on new wood for a long season. Cut back in late winter and deadhead spent heads to keep new blooms coming. Rosy-pink rounded clusters open in early summer and rebloom into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in part shade.

directions: An oakleaf selection with dense, upright flower cones packed with sterile florets that stand up well to weather. Give it well-drained soil and prune sparingly after flowering. White panicles open in early summer and turn rose-pink, with foliage reddening deeply in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; compact for smaller gardens.

directions: A compact oakleaf with upright cones that shift dramatically from white to deep rose. Its shorter stature suits small beds, and it needs only light pruning after bloom. Blooms open white in early summer and quickly deepen to ruby red, with wine-red fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in part shade.

directions: A vigorous oakleaf whose blooms turn a striking deep pink as they mature. Give it room and well-drained soil, pruning only after bloom since it flowers on old wood. Large white panicles open in early summer and age to rich pink, with bold fall foliage color.

planting.note: Plant in spring; compact habit suits containers.

directions: A dwarf, mildew-resistant mophead that stays neat without pruning. Grow it in rich, moist soil with afternoon shade and adjust pH to steer the color pink or blue. Full mophead heads open in early summer and age to attractive green tones.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil with part shade.

directions: A compact reblooming mophead that flowers on old and new wood for dependable color. Keep evenly moist and slightly acidic for the best blue, and deadhead to spur repeat bloom. Rounded heads open in early summer and continue into fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in full sun.

directions: A panicle hydrangea known for some of the largest flower heads in the group. Prune in late winter to build sturdy stems that can carry the heavy blooms. Enormous dense white panicles open in midsummer and take on pink tints as they age.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall; compact for small gardens.

directions: A compact panicle hydrangea with a color progression from cream to pink to red. Cut back in late winter to keep it dense and free-flowering. Cones open creamy white in midsummer and ripen through pink to strawberry red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall; dwarf and early-blooming.

directions: A dwarf, early-flowering panicle hydrangea that blooms weeks ahead of most cultivars. Prune in late winter to maintain its compact shape and vigor. White panicles open in early summer and shift to deep pink-red as the season progresses.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in full sun.

directions: An improved Limelight with a more compact habit and stronger stems that hold the cones upright. Prune in late winter to encourage the best flowering. Green summer panicles mature to a rich pink-red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in part shade; compact habit.

directions: A more compact oakleaf with densely packed upright flower cones. Plant in well-drained soil and prune only after flowering. White panicles open in early summer and blush pink, followed by deep red autumn foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; dwarf habit for small gardens.

directions: A dwarf oakleaf that stays low and mounded without pruning. Plant in well-drained soil and remove only spent or damaged wood after bloom. White cones open in early summer and age pink, with strong mahogany fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; very compact for edging and pots.

directions: A dwarf smooth hydrangea compact enough for edging and containers, blooming on new wood. Cut back in late winter and keep it evenly watered. Small white mophead clusters open in early summer and rebloom into fall on sturdy little stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in part shade; pollinator favorite.

directions: A native-type smooth hydrangea with large flat lacecap heads that draw bees and butterflies. It blooms on new wood, so cut it back in late winter. Broad white lacecaps up to a foot across open in early summer on sturdy stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, moist soil with afternoon shade.

directions: A reblooming mophead in the Endless Summer family that flowers on old and new wood. Keep it evenly moist and deadhead to encourage repeat bloom. Semi-double white mopheads open in early summer and pick up soft pink or blue blush as they mature.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, fertile soil with part shade.

directions: A reblooming lacecap with unusually large sterile florets that give a bold, flat-topped display. It flowers on old and new wood, so keep it watered and deadhead for continued bloom. Big lacecap heads in pink or blue open in early summer and repeat into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist soil with dappled shade.

directions: A mountain hydrangea grown for small mophead heads that change color richly through the season. Plant in humus-rich, moist soil and prune lightly after bloom. Rounded heads open pale and deepen to rose and red-purple, with reddish stems and fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in part shade.

directions: An oakleaf with unusual double florets whose pointed petals give the blooms a starry look. Plant in well-drained soil and prune only after flowering. White star-like double panicles open in early summer and age to pink, with deep fall color.

planting.note: Plant in early spring where its foliage color can be appreciated.

directions: Site in full sun to bring out the dusky purple-gray foliage, though it tolerates light shade. Prune out old canes at the base to keep growth arching and open. Small single pink flowers give way to showy red hips that persist into winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant in spring; this old rose suckers to form a colony.

directions: Plant in average, well-drained soil and give room for it to spread by suckers. Prune lightly just after the single early-summer flush since it blooms on old wood. The intensely fragrant semi-double crimson flowers appear once in early summer.

planting.note: Give this large species room at the back of a border.

directions: Plant in full sun with space for its tall, arching canes to develop. Prune only to remove dead wood and shape after flowering. Blood-red single flowers open in early summer, followed by distinctive flagon-shaped orange-red hips.

planting.note: Plant in spring; it suckers freely to bind sandy or poor soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, even sandy soil where its suckering habit is welcome. It thrives on neglect and tolerates wind and coastal exposure. Small creamy single flowers cover the thorny stems in late spring, followed by round blackish hips.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny bed or as low informal hedging.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture. This reliable landscape rose is disease-tolerant and forgiving of light pruning. Sprays of soft-pink double flowers repeat through the season, with hips if left unpruned.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy Buck rose bred for cold climates.

directions: Plant on its own roots where possible for maximum cold hardiness. It shrugs off disease and needs only a light spring shaping. Fragrant rose-pink semi-double flowers repeat from early summer to frost, followed by orange hips.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny bed; grows as a bushy shrub or short hedge.

directions: Plant in full sun and deadhead spent clusters to keep the display tidy. Prune moderately in early spring to maintain a rounded, floriferous bush. Clusters of pure white lightly scented flowers repeat abundantly from spring to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this English rose can grow tall in warm regions.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil and feed in spring and after the first flush. Prune to shape and, in mild climates, expect a larger arching bush. Cupped, richly fragrant yellow rosettes repeat through the season on a vigorous plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring; prized for one of the strongest fragrances among roses.

directions: Plant in fertile soil in full sun and mulch well to support strong growth. It can be grown as a shrub or trained on a low support since canes grow long. Intensely fragrant rich-pink rosettes open from early summer and repeat.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact English rose good near paths.

directions: Plant in full sun in enriched soil and keep evenly moist through summer. A low, spreading grower that benefits from deadheading to encourage repeat. Deep velvety crimson blooms carry a strong old-rose fragrance and repeat well.

planting.note: Plant in spring; among the most robust and healthy English roses.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and it will tolerate some difficult conditions. Very disease-resistant and vigorous, needing only routine spring pruning. Warm orange chalice-shaped blooms with a light tea fragrance repeat freely.

planting.note: Plant in spring; noted for outstanding disease resistance.

directions: Plant in full sun in good garden soil and water through establishment. One of the healthiest English roses, it forms a rounded, well-clothed bush. Soft-pink fragrant rosettes bloom early and repeat reliably all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact English rose for smaller gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in fertile soil and deadhead to keep flowers coming. Its low bushy habit suits the front of a border or a large container. Deep crimson rosettes with a fruity fragrance repeat generously.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a healthy, bushy modern English rose.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil enriched with compost. It forms a rounded, disease-resistant bush that flowers almost continuously. Peachy-orange buds open to soft apricot cups with a light tea fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an upright, healthy English rose.

directions: Plant in full sun in fertile, moisture-retentive soil. An upright grower with good disease resistance and steady repeat bloom. Coral-pink rosettes packed with petals carry a rich myrrh fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a rounded floribunda good for beds.

directions: Plant in full sun and deadhead to keep the compact bush flowering. It shows good disease resistance and heat tolerance. Butter-gold double flowers with a sweet licorice fragrance repeat all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hybrid musk that tolerates part shade.

directions: Plant in full sun or light shade in average soil and prune only lightly. This healthy hybrid musk forms a mounded, arching bush. Huge trusses of small single pink flowers with pale centers repeat until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; can be grown as a large shrub or short climber.

directions: Plant in full sun to light shade in fertile soil and support the arching canes if desired. A vigorous hybrid musk that tolerates some shade. Clusters of apricot-buff double flowers carry a warm tea fragrance and repeat.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a graceful, spreading hybrid musk.

directions: Plant in full sun or part shade with room for its wide arching frame. Prune lightly and thin old wood to keep it airy. Sprays of small coppery-pink rosettes appear in flushes and are strongest in cooler autumn weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a tall shrub that can be trained upright.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and support the tall canes in exposed sites. Deadhead large trusses to keep the plant tidy and blooming. Big heads of single ivory flowers open from apricot buds and repeat all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy rugosa hybrid for tough sites.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil, even sandy or coastal ground, and feed sparingly. The tough wrinkled foliage resists disease and salt spray. Highly fragrant semi-double white flowers repeat through summer on a thorny, upright bush.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an extremely cold-hardy rugosa.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil and expect it to sucker into a colony. Very hardy and disease-resistant, it needs little attention. Fragrant mauve-purple double flowers repeat all summer, followed by large tomato-red hips.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a large, fragrant rugosa hybrid.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil where its size and scent can be enjoyed. Tough, disease-resistant foliage and a vigorous suckering habit make it a fine informal hedge. Intensely fragrant crimson-purple double flowers repeat through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a compact rugosa with fine hips.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and leave flowers to set hips. This lower-growing rugosa is very hardy and disease-free. Single silvery-pink flowers repeat all summer, giving way to large scarlet hips and good autumn color.

planting.note: Plant in spring; bred for extreme cold hardiness.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; it tolerates poor conditions and bitter winters. The nearly thornless red-tinted canes give winter interest. Fragrant lilac-pink double flowers open early and repeat lightly through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy Canadian Explorer rose.

directions: Plant on its own roots for winter survival and mulch the crown in cold zones. Very hardy and disease-resistant, it blooms almost continuously. Bright red double flowers repeat from early summer until hard frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy Explorer that can be grown as a pillar.

directions: Plant in full sun and train the long canes on a support if a climber effect is wanted. Extremely cold-hardy and disease-resistant on its own roots. Clusters of medium red-purple double flowers bloom heavily in early summer with lighter repeat.

planting.note: Plant in spring; among the hardiest of the Explorer roses.

directions: Plant in full sun with room for a very large shrub or pillar and support if trained upward. It is exceptionally cold-hardy and shrugs off blackspot. Sprays of deep pink semi-double flowers bloom profusely in summer with repeat into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy Canadian Parkland rose.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and mulch in the coldest zones. This compact, hardy shrub blooms over a very long season. Soft blush-pink double flowers fade to ivory and repeat from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grows large in warm-climate gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and give it space to reach shrub size in mild regions. It is nearly ever-blooming and tolerates heat well. Single flowers open buff-yellow and age through pink to crimson, so several colors appear at once.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a long-blooming heirloom China rose.

directions: Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and prune lightly to shape. This heat-tolerant old rose flowers over an exceptionally long season. Semi-double pink flowers that deepen in cool weather repeat from spring nearly to winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring; the bush form stays compact and twiggy.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and prune lightly to keep it shapely. The polyantha bush form is dainty and free-flowering. Perfectly formed miniature shell-pink buds open on airy sprays and repeat through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a thornless Bourbon that tolerates part shade.

directions: Plant in full sun or a part-shaded wall and train the near-thornless canes on a support. Watch for blackspot and keep it well fed and mulched. Fragrant cerise-pink semi-double flowers repeat from early summer to autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a large Bourbon famed for fragrance.

directions: Plant in rich soil in full sun and train the long canes on a support for best display. Feed and mulch generously and watch for blackspot. Very large, intensely fragrant deep-pink double flowers are best in the autumn flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a classic once-blooming gallica.

directions: Plant in average, well-drained soil and prune only after flowering since it blooms on old wood. It suckers gently and is very cold-hardy. Large, flat, quartered crimson-purple flowers open in a single fragrant early-summer flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a striped gallica sport of the apothecary rose.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and prune lightly right after bloom since flowers form on old wood. This compact gallica suckers to form a patch. Semi-double flowers striped pink and crimson-white open once in early summer and are richly fragrant.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a low groundcover rose for edging and slopes.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and space for the low mounds to knit together. Disease-resistant and self-cleaning, it needs only a light spring trim. Soft peach double flowers repeat continuously from late spring to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an upright English rose with old-rose color.

directions: Plant in full sun in rich soil and feed after each flush of bloom. It forms an upright bush with better health than the original William Shakespeare. Deep velvety crimson rosettes carry a strong old-rose fragrance and repeat well.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a repeat-blooming Portland rose.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun and deadhead to prompt repeat bloom. This upright Portland stays compact and shrubby. Full, quartered pink flowers pack a powerful damask fragrance and repeat through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a hardy once-blooming alba rose.

directions: Plant in full sun to part shade in average soil; alba roses tolerate poorer sites than most. Prune only after flowering since bloom forms on old wood. Quartered pink rosettes with a strong fragrance open in a single midsummer flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a classic once-blooming damask rose.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun and prune after the summer flush. Support the canes if they arch under the weight of bloom. Pure white quartered flowers with a small green eye and rich fragrance open once in early summer.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring for strongest first-season growth.

directions: Dig a wide hole and spread the roots over a mound of loosened soil, setting the graft at the correct depth for your climate. Give it steady moisture and remove spent blooms to keep new buds coming. Its deep red, richly scented flowers appear on long stems from early summer onward.

planting.note: Set out in early spring in a spot with good air movement.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and water at the base to keep foliage dry and disease-free. Prune to an open vase shape in late winter and deadhead through the season. The cream blooms edged in strawberry red deepen their color in full sun and carry a strong spicy fragrance.

planting.note: Plant bare-root stock in early spring while still dormant.

directions: Position the plant in full sun with room for air to circulate, and firm the soil gently around the roots. Mulch to conserve moisture and feed regularly for repeat flushes. The velvety dark red blooms are intensely fragrant and appear from early summer through autumn.

planting.note: Set out in early spring in a warm, sheltered position.

directions: Plant in rich soil, spreading the roots and watering thoroughly to settle them. Cut back by about a third in late winter and remove faded flowers promptly. The coral-red, powerfully scented blooms repeat well from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring after the worst cold has passed.

directions: Set the roots on a firm soil mound and backfill, keeping the graft at the proper depth for your zone. Water deeply once a week and feed through summer for continuous bloom. The glowing orange flowers stand out on long cutting stems from early summer onward.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun and fertile soil.

directions: Loosen the planting hole well and work in compost before setting the roots. Keep evenly watered and deadhead to extend the show. The ruffled, coppery-buff blooms are fragrant and appear reliably from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil with the graft union set for your climate, and water in well. Prune in late winter and feed after each flush of bloom. The clean white flowers hold up well in rain and cut beautifully for the vase.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny, open bed.

directions: Spread the roots over firm soil and water thoroughly to eliminate air pockets. Give steady moisture and cut back spent blooms for repeat flowering. The pale ivory flowers blushed with pink open from early summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant.

directions: Dig a generous hole, mound soil for the roots, and set the graft at the right depth for your zone. Water deeply and mulch to keep the roots cool. The pink blooms shaded with gold at the base are strongly scented and repeat through the season.

planting.note: Set out in early spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil and firm the earth around the roots before a deep first watering. Feed regularly and remove faded blooms to encourage more. The very dark red, heavily fragrant flowers appear on tall, upright canes.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Choose a warm site, set the roots over firm soil, and water in well. Prune in late winter and watch for mildew in humid weather. The dark crimson, velvety blooms are among the most fragrant of all roses.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Plant with the roots spread and the graft positioned for your climate, then water deeply. Feed through the season and cut long stems for the vase. The coral-pink blooms blend to cream and hold their high-centered form for days.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in fertile soil.

directions: Set the plant into well-worked soil and water thoroughly to settle the roots. This vigorous, disease-resistant variety needs only routine feeding and deadheading. The soft primrose-yellow blooms fade to cream and appear freely all season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny bed.

directions: Plant in rich soil and water in well, keeping the crown at the right depth. This single-flowered heirloom blooms reliably with light pruning and steady feeding. The five soft-pink petals framing maroon stamens open from spring through fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant.

directions: Loosen the soil deeply, set the roots over a firm mound, and water thoroughly. Feed after each bloom cycle and remove spent flowers to keep it producing. The deep rose-pink blooms carry a strong old-rose fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Plant into fertile, well-drained ground and water deeply after setting the roots. Prune to an open center in late winter and deadhead through summer. The porcelain-white blooms brushed with pale pink are sweetly scented.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water in well, spacing plants for good airflow. Feed through the season and remove spent trusses to prolong bloom. Large clusters of deep crimson flowers weigh down the branches from early summer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny spot.

directions: Set into good soil and water deeply to establish the roots. This compact floribunda flowers freely with routine feeding and deadheading. The bright, non-fading yellow blooms are richly fragrant and repeat all season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in fertile soil, water in, and mulch to retain moisture. Prune lightly in late winter and deadhead for continued flowering. The ruffled lavender blooms give off a strong citrus-sweet perfume.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in an open, sunny bed.

directions: Set out in well-drained soil and water thoroughly to settle the roots. This vigorous, disease-resistant variety needs only light pruning and steady moisture. The semi-double ivory blooms edged in red bloom in generous clusters all season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and water in well. This rounded, easy-care floribunda blooms almost nonstop with routine deadheading. The butter-gold flowers have a licorice-and-spice fragrance and excellent heat tolerance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny location.

directions: Set into good soil, water deeply, and mulch around the base. This disease-resistant floribunda blooms freely with only basic care. The unusual smoky red-and-lavender blooms appear in abundant clusters from spring to frost.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water in thoroughly to establish. Feed regularly and deadhead to keep blooms coming. The unusual smoky chocolate-orange flowers are lightly fragrant and repeat all season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set the plant into fertile soil and water deeply after planting. Prune lightly in late winter and remove faded blooms through the season. The deep smoky-purple, clove-scented flowers deepen in cooler weather.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny bed.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water in well to settle the roots. This low, spreading floribunda flowers freely with routine feeding and deadheading. The full amber-yellow blooms are fragrant and repeat throughout the season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set into fertile soil and water thoroughly to establish the roots. Feed after each flush and deadhead to keep new buds forming. The blush-white blooms open from pearly buds and carry an outstanding sweet fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water in well after setting the roots. This variety prefers a sheltered spot in colder zones and steady moisture. The dainty ivory blooms flushed with apricot appear in graceful sprays all season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set into fertile soil and water deeply to settle the roots. Feed regularly and remove spent blooms to prolong flowering. The yellow blooms edged in red are strongly fragrant and repeat well through the season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny bed.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water in well to establish. This compact, disease-resistant floribunda needs only light pruning and deadheading. The bright orange-red blooms cover the low bush from spring to frost.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set into fertile, well-drained soil and water thoroughly. Feed through the growing season and deadhead spent trusses. Large sprays of soft pink, camellia-like blooms smother the plant repeatedly through the season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny location.

directions: Plant in good soil and water deeply after setting the roots. This healthy floribunda flowers freely with basic feeding and deadheading. The single blooms blend scarlet, orange, and gold with a hint of honey scent.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set into well-drained soil and water in well to establish the roots. This low, mounded floribunda blooms heavily with only light care. Masses of bright scarlet-red flowers cover the compact bush all season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; give this tall grower room.

directions: Plant in fertile soil with plenty of space, and water in deeply. This vigorous grandiflora can reach shrub size, so prune to control height and feed generously. Clear pink blooms appear singly and in clusters on long stems all season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set into fertile, well-drained soil and water thoroughly to settle the roots. This tall grandiflora rewards regular feeding and deadheading with long stems for cutting. The golden-yellow blooms brushed with orange appear freely all season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny spot.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water in well after spreading the roots. This tall, vigorous grandiflora responds to feeding with long cutting stems. The bicolor blooms show golden-bronze inside and deep russet-red outside.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set into fertile soil and water deeply to establish the roots. Feed through the season and remove spent blooms to keep it flowering. Each burgundy-and-white striped bloom is uniquely marked and richly perfumed.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sunny bed.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water in well to establish. This healthy floribunda flowers freely with routine feeding and deadheading. The blooms blend soft yellow, apricot, and pink and carry a light sweet fragrance.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set into fertile soil and water deeply after planting. Feed regularly and deadhead to encourage repeat flushes. The ruffled blooms glow in shades of apricot, peach, and melon all season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring while dormant.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil and water in thoroughly. This sport of the classic Peace rose needs the same routine feeding and late-winter pruning. The large blooms blend deep pink, copper, and gold and repeat through the season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set the roots over firm soil and water in well, keeping the graft at the right depth. Feed after each bloom cycle and deadhead spent flowers. The high-centered white blooms show a faint green cast in cool weather and are lightly scented.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: Plant in fertile soil and water in well, choosing a warm site for best health. Feed regularly and watch for mildew in humid conditions. The amber-gold blooms are strongly fragrant and repeat through the season.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in full sun.

directions: Set into well-drained soil and water deeply to settle the roots. Feed through the season and remove faded blooms to prolong flowering. The lilac-mauve, high-centered flowers carry a strong sweet fragrance.

planting.note: Set out bare-root or container plants in early spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Dig a wide hole and position the bud union at or just below the soil line, then water deeply to settle the roots. Tie the long, pliable canes horizontally to a fence or trellis to coax more flowering side shoots. Expect flushes of soft pale-pink blooms from late spring through fall on this repeat-flowering climber.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot with good airflow to limit mildew.

directions: Plant this nearly thornless Bourbon where its cerise-pink, strongly fragrant flowers can be enjoyed up close, such as beside a doorway. Prune out weak wood in late winter and tie in new canes to a support. Deadhead spent blooms to encourage repeat flushes through the season.

planting.note: Establish in spring against a sunny wall or pillar.

directions: Plant with the graft union at the soil surface and mulch to keep the roots cool. Train the canes across a wall or trellis and give only light pruning, since hard cutting reduces bloom. Clusters of pure white flowers appear almost continuously from early summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring where it gets full sun for best color.

directions: Set the plant in rich soil and water it in well, keeping the roots evenly moist through the first season. Fan the canes out on a trellis and remove spent blooms to keep new buds coming. The velvety deep-red, fragrant flowers repeat through summer and make excellent cut roses.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a sturdy support that can carry the heavy blooms.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and provide a strong trellis, as the full old-fashioned blooms are weighty when wet. Tie canes as they grow and prune lightly after the main flush. Large cupped flowers of cream flushed with pink open from spring and repeat into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring along a fence line for a strong show of color.

directions: Dig a generous hole, spread the roots, and firm the soil around the base. Train the vigorous canes horizontally and prune out old wood after flowering to renew the plant. Bright scarlet clusters cover the plant in early summer with a lighter repeat later on.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates a little shade better than most climbers.

directions: Plant in fertile soil and water regularly during establishment. Tie the fairly stiff, upright canes to a pillar or wall and deadhead to prolong bloom. Loosely double, lightly scented yellow flowers open from late spring and repeat well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the richest coral tones.

directions: Set out the plant with roots well spread and mulch after planting. Guide the canes onto a trellis and remove faded blooms to keep it flowering. Fragrant coral-salmon blooms recur through the season and are good for cutting.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a pillar, arch, or wall in full sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained ground and keep watered through the first summer. This restrained climber suits smaller supports, so tie in the canes and prune lightly. Deep blood-red flowers hold their color well and repeat reliably all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the boldest striping.

directions: Plant with the graft union near the surface and mulch to conserve moisture. Train the vigorous canes to a fence and shear off spent trusses to spur rebloom. Semi-double blooms striped red and white with a sweet scent appear in repeated flushes.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm wall in cooler zones.

directions: Plant in a sheltered, sunny position and water in well. Tie the canes to a support and prune only to shape after the first bloom. Flat, quartered creamy-white flowers with a strong tea fragrance repeat through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring where its fragrance can be appreciated.

directions: Plant into enriched soil and mulch generously after watering in. The sturdy branching habit suits a wall or pillar, so tie in the canes and deadhead often. Shapely apricot-pink blooms carry a rich sweet scent and repeat through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the crispest picotee edge.

directions: Plant in a well-drained bed and keep the roots moist while establishing. Train onto a trellis or wall and prune lightly to keep the framework open. Cream flowers edged in rosy pink appear in flushes from early summer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; works as a short climber or large shrub.

directions: Set out with roots spread and firm the soil to remove air pockets. Tie the stiff canes to a low support or let it stand as a shrub, pruning lightly. Full, fragrant rose-pink blooms with darker reverses repeat dependably through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to bring out the changing colors.

directions: Plant in fertile soil and water deeply through the first season. Train the moderate canes to a fence or pillar and deadhead to keep new color coming. Blooms open yellow and shift through orange to red, giving a multicolored effect on one plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the most flowers.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil and mulch after settling the roots. Tie the canes to a support and remove faded flowers to encourage a steady succession. Large single, blood-red blooms with golden stamens repeat from early summer and draw pollinators.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates a north-facing wall.

directions: Plant in improved soil and water in well, then mulch around the base. This vigorous Noisette copes with some shade, so tie the long canes to a wall and prune lightly. Blush-white, sweetly scented blooms appear in generous flushes from early summer onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a warm, sheltered wall.

directions: Plant in a warm site with good drainage and water regularly while young. Train the canes on wires and give only light pruning to keep flowering wood. Quartered buff-yellow to apricot blooms with a strong tea scent open early and repeat through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a large, strong structure in a mild climate.

directions: Give this immense, nearly thornless rose a pergola or sturdy wall and plant in well-drained soil. Once established it is drought-tolerant and needs pruning only after flowering. Masses of small, soft-yellow double blooms smother the plant for several weeks in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring and give it room to spread on a wall.

directions: Plant this large English rose where its long arching canes can be trained onto a support. Prune after the single flush and tie in new growth for next year. Big, cupped clear-pink blooms with a strong myrrh fragrance appear once, in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a short climber or large shrub.

directions: Plant into rich, well-drained soil and mulch after watering in. Train the healthy canes onto a low wall or pillar and deadhead to encourage repeat bloom. Rosette blooms of soft peachy pink with a fruity fragrance recur through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring on an arch, obelisk, or wall.

directions: Plant in fertile soil and keep evenly watered during establishment. Train the graceful canes onto a support and prune lightly after each flush. Soft pink, many-petaled blooms with a musk-and-myrrh scent repeat well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; give it a large, strong support.

directions: Plant this very vigorous sport where it has plenty of room, such as into a tree or over a large pergola. Prune only to control size after the main flush. Sprays of tiny, perfectly formed pale-pink buds open over a long early-summer season with a lighter repeat.

planting.note: Plant in spring against a pale wall to set off the dark blooms.

directions: Plant in enriched soil and water in thoroughly, then mulch. Tie the canes to a support and prune lightly to keep flowering wood. Very dark, velvety crimson-maroon blooms carry an intense damask fragrance, mainly in an early-summer flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates a partly shaded wall.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil and keep watered while establishing. Train the moderate canes to a wall or fence and deadhead spent blooms. Bright orange-scarlet flowers repeat through summer on a tough, adaptable plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a pergola or long fence with airflow.

directions: Give this vigorous rambler a large support and plant in well-drained soil with good air movement. Prune out old flowered canes after bloom and tie in fresh growth. Large trusses of single, deep-pink flowers with white eyes cover the plant once in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in an open, airy spot to reduce mildew.

directions: Plant into good soil and give the long, flexible canes an arch or fence to scramble over. Cut out old wood after flowering and tie in the new canes. Cascades of small, double rose-pink flowers appear in one long midsummer display.

planting.note: Plant in spring on a sturdy structure with room to spread.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and provide a strong support for the thick, thorny canes. Thin out old wood after bloom to keep the plant open and healthy. Coppery-pink buds open to fragrant salmon-pink flowers in a single, abundant summer flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring near a large tree it can climb into.

directions: This enormous rambler is best planted to scramble through a sturdy tree or over a big structure. Once established it needs little pruning beyond removing dead wood. Huge, airy sprays of small blush-pink pompon flowers cascade down in a single early-summer show.

planting.note: Plant in spring where it can grow large and unrestrained.

directions: Site this vigorous rambler where it can climb a tree or cover an outbuilding, and plant in ordinary well-drained soil. Minimal pruning is needed once it is established. Clusters of small, semi-double creamy-white flowers smother the plant in summer and are followed by small red hips.

planting.note: Plant in spring; copes with a shadier aspect than most.

directions: Plant this tough, near-evergreen rambler in well-drained soil and give it a wall or tree to climb. Remove old flowered wood after bloom to keep it tidy. Tight rosette buds of pale pink open to small creamy-white pompon flowers in a single summer flush.
